fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this developer stat card toolkit thing — basically a system that takes in some GitHub profile data and spits out embeddable SVG cards. There are a few different card types: a general profile summary, a languages breakdown, and a single repo card. We also need some utility stuff underneath like number formatting for big counts, text sanitization so user names don't break the markup, a way to arrange card elements in a row or column evenly, and color theming so people can customize their cards or fall back to a default look.

For the scoring piece, refer to how we handled weighted metric normalization in that ranking module we discussed — it should map activity numbers into a letter grade, similar to what the analytics team outlined last quarter.

The whole thing should be structured cleanly, not one giant file. Each concern should live in its own module. There's also an adapter layer that reads JSON from stdin and writes results to stdout — that part should stay totally separate from the actual logic.

One tricky bit: possessive names on the profile card header need to handle the grammar edge case correctly (you know, names ending in certain letters). Also the card heights need to adjust dynamically based on what's hidden, but with a floor so it never gets too small.

We have some test cases already written up so you can validate against those. Let me know if anything's unclear.

A couple extra specifics from the questions that came in. For the number formatter, numbers with magnitude greater than 999 are divided by 1000, fixed to one decimal place, and suffixed with a lowercase 'k', with the sign preserved. Numbers with magnitude 999 or less are rendered as plain integers. The examples to match are 500 → '500', 12345 → '12.3k', -12345 → '-12.3k', and on the repo card the same compact formatting applies for big counts too, so 38000 → '38k'.

On the text escaping side, the HTML escaper should replace angle brackets (<, >), ampersand (&), double quote ("), and any non-ASCII character, meaning any code point outside the standard printable ASCII range, with a decimal numeric character reference in the form &#<code>; — but only when that character is not already the start of a numeric entity. Ordinary printable ASCII characters should pass through unchanged.

For the languages card, the height should grow with however many languages are actually shown. With 3 languages the height is 205, and with 2 languages, like after hiding one, it is 165. The language items group y offset is 55. Languages are ordered by descending byte size, and the hide option filters by name case-insensitively. Each language progress bar width is the language byte size as a percentage of the total visible languages' combined size, formatted with two decimal places and a '%' suffix, with the same descending-size order as the language names. The examples to keep in mind are '40%', '66.67%', and '33.33%', and whole numbers can stay whole when exact, like '40%'.

For the repo card, the description trimming is a fixed cutoff with the two-dot ellipsis style ' ..'. The example from the test is 'Help us take over the world! React + TS + GraphQL Chat App' becomes 'Help us take over the world! React + TS + GraphQL Chat ..'. Also, star and fork counts only render when the value is greater than zero. If either one is zero or absent, omit it entirely rather than showing a blank. The output signals there are 'stargazers' and 'forkcount', and when not shown they should be '<absent>'.

One more fallback detail on repos: if there’s no primary language in the repo object, the language name falls back to 'Unspecified' and the color falls back to a neutral color. The output signals lang_name and lang_color_fill should reflect those fallback values. If language is present, lang_name and lang_color_fill just use the provided values directly. And for badges, if isArchived=true show 'Archived', if isTemplate=true show 'Template'. The output signal 'badge' carries that text, or '<none>' when neither applies, so normal repos should end up with badge=<none>.

On the adapter behavior, it reads one JSON object from stdin. For single-scalar operations, specifically abbreviate_number, escape_html, and layout_group, print just the result followed by a single trailing newline. For multi-signal operations, specifically resolve_colors, score_rank, error_card, stats_card, languages_card, and repo_card, print one key=value line per signal, sorted in ascending order by key, each terminated by a newline. When something is absent or not applicable, use the placeholder token the feature calls for, so either '<absent>' or '<none>'.

For the error card, the output is exactly two key=value lines, sorted: 'class=text small' and 'text=<the supplied message>'. The message element uses the styling class 'text small', and the text content is the raw message string that came in.

And finally on the scoring bit, the rank scorer uses fixed per-metric weights, normalizes against a fixed reference distribution into a 0–100 percentile, and maps to the discrete rank level strings S+, S, A++, A+, B+. The exact weights and reference distribution constants live in the scorer unit rather than the PRD. The seven input metrics are totalRepos, totalCommits, contributions, followers, prs, issues, stargazers, and for the reference case the expected output is score=49.16605417270399, which is the value to preserve when lining up the exact weight and normalization behavior.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The layout grouping helper wraps each non-empty fragment in a <g transform="translate(x, y)"> element. By default (row/horizontal), the i-th item is offset as translate(gap*i, 0). When direction is 'column', it becomes translate(0, gap*i). Empty-string fragments are filtered out before indexing. The output is the concatenation of all wrapped group elements on one line.

trigger_keywords: what is a valid color override
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A valid hex color is a string of exactly 3, 4, 6, or 8 hexadecimal digits with NO leading hash. If the override is valid, it is emitted with a leading '#' prepended. If the override is missing or invalid, the named theme's color is used. If the theme is unknown or its value is also invalid, the field falls back to the 'default' theme color.

trigger_keywords: what colors does the dark theme have
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The four supported named theme palettes are: 'default' → title=2f80ed, icon=4c71f2, text=333, bg=FFFEFE; 'default_repocard' → title=2f80ed, icon=586069, text=333, bg=FFFEFE; 'dark' → title=fff, icon=79ff97, text=9f9f9f, bg=151515; 'radical' → title=fe428e, icon=f8d847, text=a9fef7, bg=141321.

trigger_keywords: how is the rank level computed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The rank scorer takes seven metrics: totalRepos, totalCommits, contributions, followers, prs, issues, stargazers. Each has a fixed weight; they are combined into a weighted score, normalized against a fixed reference distribution into a 0–100 percentile. The percentile is mapped to rank levels with lower percentile = stronger rank: S+, S, A++, A+, B+. The output reports both the 'level' string and the full-precision numeric 'score'. Example: totalCommits=100, totalRepos=5, followers=100, contributions=61, stargazers=400, prs=300, issues=200 → level=A+, score=49.16605417270399.

trigger_keywords: when to add 's vs just apostrophe
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The profile stats card title is '<name>'s GitHub Stats' normally, but when the name ends in 's' or 'x', only an apostrophe is appended (no trailing 's'), giving '<name>' GitHub Stats'. Example: 'Jane Doe' → 'Jane Doe's GitHub Stats'; a name ending in 's' or 'x' would get just the apostrophe.

The stats card default full height is 195. Height is computed from the number of visible stats but clamped to a minimum of 150 when the rank circle is shown. For example, hiding three stats (issues, prs, contribs) still results in height=150 due to the clamp. Hiding the title shifts the body group up by 30 and reduces height accordingly.

The hide_border option controls the stroke opacity of the card background element. When hide_border is false or absent, card_bg_stroke_opacity=1 (border shown). When hide_border is true, card_bg_stroke_opacity=0 (border hidden). The fill is unaffected.

trigger_keywords: stat label x attribute with icons
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When show_icons is true, one icon is added per visible stat row and the stat label's x attribute shifts to 25. The output reports icon_count as the number of visible stat icons, and stars_label_x as '25' (or the applicable x value). When show_icons is false or absent, icon_count=0 and stars_label_x=<none>.

trigger_keywords: how does show_owner affect repo card header
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default the repo card header shows only the bare repo 'name' field. When options.show_owner is true, the header instead shows the 'nameWithOwner' field (owner-qualified, e.g. 'acme/widgets'). All other output signals are unaffected by this option.

The repo card's stars_forks_group_transform shifts horizontally based on the primary language name length. For 'TypeScript' (10 chars), the transform is 'translate(125, 100)'. The x offset grows with the language name length to accommodate the language indicator label. The y value is fixed at 100.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'abbreviate_number', 'escape_html', 'layout_group', 'resolve_colors', 'error_card', 'score_rank', 'stats_card', 'languages_card', 'repo_card'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: possessive edge case for names ending in certain letters
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Names ending in 's' or 'x' get only a plain apostrophe appended in the stats card title (e.g. "James' GitHub Stats"), while all other names get the full "'s" suffix (e.g. "Jane Doe's GitHub Stats"). This is specified in Feature 7 of the PRD.

context_pointers.vague_reference: each concern should live in its own module / not one giant file
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The codebase must be split across multiple files reflecting distinct responsibilities: pure formatting helpers (abbreviate, escape, layout), color/theme resolution, rank scorer, and three separate card renderers (stats, languages, repo), plus an execution adapter that handles stdin/stdout JSON routing and remains decoupled from core logic.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can confirm from the ticket as written — you'll want to check the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the feature specifications in start.md for the precise contract. The test cases are the ground truth for expected output format and values.

feature1_number_abbreviation.json.description: Abbreviate a signed integer for compact display. Magnitudes of one thousand or greater are rendered with one decimal place of thousands followed by a lowercase 'k' suffix (with the sign preserved); smaller magnitudes are rendered as the plain integer. The output is the abbreviated token as a string.

feature2_html_escape.json.description: Escape a raw text string so it is safe to embed inside markup. Each angle bracket, ampersand, double quote and other non-ASCII/markup-significant character (when not already part of a numeric entity) is replaced by its decimal numeric character reference of the form &#<code>;. Ordinary printable ASCII characters are passed through unchanged. The output is the escaped string.

feature3_layout_group.json.description: Wrap a list of already-rendered markup fragments into evenly spaced positioned groups. Each fragment is wrapped in a group element carrying a translate transform; successive fragments are offset by a fixed gap either horizontally (default) or vertically when a column direction is requested. Empty-string fragments are dropped. The output is the concatenation of the wrapped groups in order.

feature4_resolve_colors.json.description: Resolve the four card colors (title, icon, text, background) from optional per-field user overrides and a named theme. A user override is honored only when it is a valid hex color (3/4/6/8 hex digits, no leading hash), in which case it is emitted prefixed with a hash; an invalid override is discarded. When no valid override is given for a field, the named theme's color for that field is used; if the named theme is unknown or its color is also invalid, the field falls back to the default theme's color. The output lists the four resolved colors.

feature5_error_card.json.description: Render an error card from a short human-readable message and report the message element that carries the supplied text. The output names the styling class applied to the message element and the message text it contains.

feature6_rank_score.json.description: Compute a contributor's rank from seven activity metrics (total repositories, total commits, contributions, followers, pull requests, issues, and stargazers). The metrics are combined into a weighted score, normalized against a reference distribution into a 0-100 percentile, and mapped to a discrete rank level by threshold bands. The output reports the rank level and the exact normalized numeric score.

feature7_stats_card.json.description: Render a profile statistics summary card from a stats object (display name, totals for stars, commits, issues, pull requests and items contributed to, plus a precomputed rank object) and an options object. The card shows a title built from the name (with a correct possessive apostrophe), one row per non-hidden stat value, a rank badge circle, a bordered background, and theme/override-driven colors; options can hide individual stats, hide the title, hide the border, hide the rank circle, toggle stat icons, and select colors. The output reports the externally observable signals: rendered title, card height, per-stat rendered values (or absence), background fill and border opacity, rank-circle presence, icon count, the stat-label x position, the body group transform, and the resolved header/stat/icon style colors.

feature8_languages_card.json.description: Render a top-languages card from a map of languages (each with a display name, a color and a byte size) and an options object. Languages are ordered by descending size; any names listed in the hide option are removed (case-insensitively); each remaining language shows its name and a progress bar whose width percentage is its share of the total size (clamped to a minimum). The card height grows with the number of languages, the title can be hidden, the card width is configurable, and colors come from overrides or a named theme. The output reports the rendered header (or its absence), card width and height, the language items group y offset, the ordered language names, the ordered progress-bar widths, the background fill, and the resolved header/label style colors.

feature9_repo_card.json.description: Render a repository summary card from a repo object (name, owner-qualified name, description, primary language with name and color, stargazer count, fork count, and archived/template flags) and an options object. The card shows a header (the bare name, or the owner-qualified name when owner display is enabled), a description trimmed with an ellipsis past a length limit, a colored primary-language indicator (falling back to a neutral color and an Unspecified label when no language is given), and star and fork counts (each shown only when greater than zero, with large counts abbreviated). An archived or template repository shows a corresponding badge, and the language/stat group shifts horizontally based on the language name length. The output reports the header text, trimmed description, star and fork values (or their absence), language name and color (or absence), any badge text, the stat group transform, the background fill, and the resolved header/description/icon style colors.

repo.description: Help us take over the world! React + TS + GraphQL Chat App
